author: @ryanlebeck259
text:
THAT is actually called a barrel rotor and is a little used type of horizontal axis rotor. It is seen more in hydroelectric turbines than in wind based models.

Well done you 😊.

author: @speed2998
text: That was a cute demonstration, but how does the performance compare with the conventional propeller-type turbine?  Your next step would be to attach a generator such as the PM brush type DC machine (I don't think a stepper motor would make an efficient generator) and measure the output power.  Virtually all of the cylindrical turbines suffer from the drag of the blades that are moving upwind, even if they are feathered.  But keep up the work.

author: @stevezeika2901
text:
Unfortunately, Sintra Board is not the same as Builder Board. Sintra Board is a high density, expanded, unplasticised (ridged) PVC foam of uniform density throughout its thickness—in other words it is NOT a structural sandwich like Builder Board and is substantially heavier and less stiff. It is also more expensive, difficult to find locally (sign shop would be best bet), and not available in large sheets (2’ x 4’ seems to be the largest), and harder to work with (you can’t score and crack and it’s weight and floppiness make it difficult to maneuver and affix).
I have spent many fruitless hours searching Builder Board or something comparable in the US to no avail. When it comes to innovative building materials available to hobbyists, the US can be pretty backward (we build pretty much all of our housing out of inflammable fungus food harvested from tree carcasses).

replies.author: @jimmacaulay844
replies.text: Not just foam core?  Like picture mounting board?

replies.author: @stevezeika2901
replies.text: Foamcore demonstrates the principle of structural sandwiches, but it uses ordinary paper as its facing, whereas Builder Board uses rigid PVC, which is much stronger and waterproof. It also has much more forgiving failure characteristics because it deforms under overload, while paper tears causing a sudden structural failure. Because paper absorbs moisture from the air and changes dimensions, it has poor shape stability over time. Finally, come core is widely available in only 1/8” (6mm) thickness; Builder Board is many thickness up to at least 1/2” (12mm). The stiffness of a sandwich varies as the third power of the thickness and the strength as the square, so this is huge difference structurally.

replies.author: @jimmacaulay844
replies.text: You sound like a structural engineer - me too.  The foam core we can get at craft stores in the US is ¼", and I've seen ½", but rarely ⅛".  Wish I knew where to get this builder's board.

replies.author: @TerryGilsenan
replies.text: @MyNameIsLukey You could use more of that builders board and make some 3 phase planar alternators. These would have much less reactive residence to rotation than the stepper motors. They would also be very simple to make and you just need to get some rare-earth magnets. With a 120mm circle of builders board with the magnets arranged in alternate polarity at say 15 degree spacing connected to the bottle and free to spin, and a second circle of builders board with coils spaced at say 20 degrees attached to the "frame" (out of phase magnet to coil, will reduce the reactive drag impulse)

author: @Cooliemasteroz
text: Hi Luke that’s a nice quick project. The problem with some wind turbines is that they are often high speed and low torque and power is lost with the smallest amount of friction therefore if you are not powering an alternator directly and just want to power something mechanical you will need an efficient gear reduction. The first thing that comes to mind is worm gears but sometimes they can be inconvenient as you end up with shafts at 90 degrees. There is another interesting efficient gear reduction and it’s called a harmonic drive it might be a challenge to make an efficient one but it might be just fun to make one to see how it works. It should be easy to find on the net.

author: @peterdkay
text:
1. Try vertical axis so wind direction is not critical. 
2. Strip out bearing seals to reduce friction.
3. Stepper motor has too much holding torque to start. Try small DC motor.

author: @KerrFamilyLaw
text: Hi Luke - nice to see you on the main channel. I am a noob to all this physics and amazing stuff and while I am fascinated by Robert’s stuff, I can actually understand what you are doing so that’s great. I would like to know where I can scavenge a stepper motor and I would have liked to know the “materials” list right at the beginning.  I actually need to implement a “heated ink floor”,   (and hopefully walls too) and actually I’d like to heat my fresh water and black and grey tanks using some sort of “ink radiator” or “ink blanket”. I live in Canada and I will be wintering in my RV. Last winter was my first and I was not satisfied with my solutions for cold relief (mainly a very powerful heated mattress cover on every horizontal surface and lots of time staying in bed). I put 2 inch blue rigid styrofoam on the floors and central windows and while these were very comfortable and kept my feet from freezing off, 2 inches blocked all the cupboards and was very difficult to clean and it was shredded because my dog loves scratching her nails on it. But I started watching this channel and I saw these ink videos and I’m going to use the styrofoam remains for some other purpose this winter and instead implement your heated-floors-with-ink. I have a propane hook up and an electricity hook up, but I won’t for the following winter, so I am going to trial various off-grid solutions to my power and water needs this upcoming winter. But back to your video: there are very high winds regularly here, and there is unobstructed sky for some sun collection, but it is going to be Canada in the winter so sun alone will not suffice (I’m near the south end of lake Simcoe parked on a semi-rural property).   So, I am actually planning to implement working ink to heat my real space and I actually need to generate both wind and sun power and design an inexpensive but efficient and practical power storage system that will hold my power until I need it.  I am building everything from free scavenged parts, or as-inexpensive-as-possible-components, and I hope to build everything myself from first principles so by the end of a year I will be able to understand Robert. So - I will be following your videos-for-the-beginner - and using your examples to gain experience and understanding that I can use practically. I am keen to see more “Practical  Luke” videos where you show how these things you are building can actually be used - for example - once I find me a little stepper motor (probably from a broken printer I saved?) I will build your pop bottle wind turbine. I am hoping you are going to show us a practical use for it - for example to maybe attach it to a home-made mini charge controller and a home-made small lithium battery pack and a small heated  “ink pad” I can attach on or around my windows (everyone has windows that fog in the winter)). Is it possible to make a large thin, clear heated ink pad? Oh, say it is possible! I could use one to defog the windows I am not going to cover with styrofoam this winter because seeing out is more important to me than removing all opportunity for heat loss. So anyways - I think it would be very cool if you make these projects useful like your glove-heater - but simplify simplify simplify or tinify tinify tinify so beginners can understand and actually have something useful at the end of a project. And include the steps for people who can’t just go out and buy a huge pot of graphene. I will need to make my own, in considerable quantities if I am to succeed in heating my winter. But I am afraid to follow the “Robert” examples because while I suspect there are many dangers, I don’t understand them, and I am very wary - I suspect he is way too brilliant to remember ignorance - whereas I am confident that you will explain all the dangers in working with these materials and you will make sure to tell us exactly what not to do to keep everything as safe as possible, while actually demonstrating practical, useful, “keeper” projects for beginners (no matter how old we are). Thanks for making your videos - I look forward to more of them - your enthusiasm for the wonders and possibilities of science is refreshing. Also - I wonder if you might think about doing “companion” videos to Robert videos. I see one just blipped across my screen - and I am going to go and watch it and be inspired-but I am sure not to understand it enough to actually try it myself- maybe you might consider doing a “junior” version of his videos going into detail about the safety issues, basic theory-why it is such a neat idea, and showing a scaled down build or cheaper or smaller version, and of course suggest the crazy variety of actual possibilities of how these things can be used in a practical way in a real life and a real home on a human scale. Cheers.

author: @tdtrecordsmusic
text: I recommend getting used to winding wire. The kits / pre-made tech has got society ignant. Winding wire is VERY easy. It will take some years to get the specs down to a tee, but its worth getting used to. For example > wind turbine will only produce X amount of torque. AND when it gets going it has inertia... How do you match the electromotive drag to the rotational force? How many turns of what guage wire ?

author: @IsThisAvailable550
text:
You need to block off either the top or the bottom of the bottle!
Because, (example) if the wind is pushing the top, it will ALSO push the bottom, which will act as a brake.
Therefore you need to let the wind push either the top *OR* the bottom.
